rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Expunge Petitioner'S Notice Of Pendency Of Action (Lis Penden)
rulings.Rulings: Respondents Gustav Lindqvist's and Caroline Ingeborn's Motion to Expunge Petitioner's Notice of Pendency of Action (Lis Pendens) is granted. The April 30, 2018 lis pendens must be expunged. Petitioner has not met his statutory burden under CCP § 405.31 to show that that December 21, 2017 petition to probate decedent's 2015 Will contains a "real property claim." (CCP §§ § 405.4, 405.20). A petition to probate a will is not a "claim" by which a petitioner is "prosecuting another for the declaration, enforcement, or protection of a right." (CCP § 22). At most, if successful, Petitioner would gain standing to marshal and pursue property for the estate. The petitioner's appointment would not resolve any property rights the estate might claim or resolve any dispute as to the subject property. Further, the Court is required to expunge the lis pendens under CCP § 405.32 because Petitioner has not met his statutory burden to establish "by a preponderance of the evidence the probable validity of the real property claim." The case Petitioner cites to argue that the Motion should be denied because he recorded the lis pendens in good faith and for a proper purpose has been superseded by statute; the statutes the case relies on have been repealed, and do not reflect the current statutory showing necessary to defeat a motion to expunge. Petitioner included no evidence whatsoever to demonstrate he would prevail on his petition to be appointed personal representative, let alone any evidence to show that his preferred version of the competing testamentary documents would be shown valid. In light of the Court's conclusions, it is unnecessary to determine whether the lis pendens would also fail on the basis of notice. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and other parties no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, May 22, 2019.
continued to June 20, 2019 to be heard with Chicago Title Company's demurrer

Respondents Gustav Lindqvist's and Caroline Ingeborn's Motion to Expunge Petitioner's Notice of Pendency of Action (Lis Pendens) is granted. The April 30, 2018 lis pendens must be expunged. Petitioner has not met his statutory burden under CCP § 405.31 to show that that December 21, 2017 petition to probate decedent's 2015 Will contains a "real property claim." (CCP §§ § 405.4, 405.20). A petition to probate a will is not a "claim" by which a petitioner is "prosecuting another for the declaration, enforcement, or protection of a right." (CCP § 22). At most, if successful, Petitioner would gain standing to marshal and pursue property for the estate. The petitioner's appointment would not resolve any property rights the estate might claim or resolve any dispute as to the subject property. Further, the Court is required to expunge the lis pendens under CCP § 405.32 because Petitioner has not met his statutory burden to establish "by a preponderance of the evidence the probable validity of the real property claim." The case Petitioner cites to argue that the Motion should be denied because he recorded the lis pendens in good faith and for a proper purpose has been superseded by statute; the statutes the case relies on have been repealed, and do not reflect the current statutory showing necessary to defeat a motion to expunge. Petitioner included no evidence whatsoever to demonstrate he would prevail on his petition to be appointed personal representative, let alone any evidence to show that his preferred version of the competing testamentary documents would be shown valid. In light of the Court's conclusions, it is unnecessary to determine whether the lis pendens would also fail on the basis of notice. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and other parties no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, May 22, 2019.
